Name: dynamic_kernel_fusion
Title: Dynamic Kernel Fusion for Adaptive Inference Efficiency
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic kernel fusion, guided by real-time profiled hardware metrics, can significantly improve inference efficiency by adapting kernel fusion strategies to varying input characteristics and hardware conditions.
Related Work: Existing work on kernel fusion primarily focuses on static fusion strategies, which are optimized for specific hardware configurations and input patterns. Recent advancements in adaptive inference, such as mixed-precision computation and dynamic layer skipping, highlight the benefits of runtime adaptation but do not address kernel fusion. Our proposal distinguishes itself by introducing dynamic kernel fusion, which leverages real-time hardware profiling to adapt fusion strategies on-the-fly, optimizing for both latency and throughput across diverse input scenarios.
Abstract: We propose Dynamic Kernel Fusion (DKF), a novel approach to optimizing inference efficiency by adapting kernel fusion strategies in real-time based on profiled hardware metrics. Traditional static kernel fusion methods are limited by their inability to adapt to varying input characteristics and hardware conditions. DKF addresses this limitation by continuously monitoring hardware metrics such as arithmetic intensity, memory bandwidth utilization, and tensor-core occupancy, and dynamically adjusting kernel fusion strategies to optimize for latency and throughput. Our approach leverages CUDA/Triton kernels and integrates real-time profiling tools like Nsight to guide fusion decisions. We evaluate DKF on commodity GPUs, measuring end-to-end speedups, tokens-per-second, and energy efficiency. Our experiments demonstrate that DKF achieves significant improvements in inference efficiency, particularly under varying input patterns and hardware conditions, compared to static fusion methods. By bridging model compression with deployment systems engineering, DKF offers a practical, hardware-faithful solution for efficient inference that can be deployed immediately.
Experiments: 1. Baseline Comparison: Compare the performance of DKF against static kernel fusion methods on a range of input patterns and hardware configurations, measuring latency, throughput, and energy efficiency. 2. Real-Time Profiling Validation: Validate the accuracy and effectiveness of real-time hardware profiling in guiding dynamic kernel fusion decisions. 3. Input Pattern Adaptation: Evaluate the ability of DKF to adapt to varying input patterns, including different sequence lengths and batch sizes, by measuring the corresponding changes in kernel fusion strategies and inference efficiency. 4. Hardware Condition Adaptation: Assess DKF's performance under different hardware conditions, such as varying GPU memory bandwidth and tensor-core occupancy, to demonstrate its adaptability. 5. End-to-End Speedup Measurement: Measure the end-to-end speedup achieved by DKF on real-world inference tasks, using wall-clock latency and tokens-per-second as key metrics.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. Overhead of Real-Time Profiling: The overhead introduced by real-time profiling could potentially offset the gains from dynamic kernel fusion, particularly for lightweight models or small batch sizes. 2. Complexity of Kernel Fusion Strategies: The complexity of dynamically adjusting kernel fusion strategies may increase the implementation complexity and require sophisticated tuning. 3. Hardware-Specific Optimization: The effectiveness of DKF may vary across different hardware architectures, requiring additional tuning for each specific GPU model. 4. Latency Variability: The dynamic nature of kernel fusion may introduce variability in latency, which could be problematic for applications requiring strict latency guarantees.
